positioning.headline: Neutral regime → Neutral stance (balanced).
positioning.narrative: The macro regime is Neutral, mapping to the Neutral allocation stance (balanced). Growth assets are 55.3% of the book. At the tactical layer, the only active bets are Intl Developed overweight 1.5pp, EM Equity overweight 1.5pp, Real Assets overweight 1.5pp, Cash & Alts underweight 4.5pp, self-funded across sleeves. The Layer 4 risk envelope is inactive — forecast volatility sits comfortably below budget (36-month forecast vol 9.1% vs the 14.0% Neutral budget).

bets.text: Intl Developed overweight 1.5pp — DCS 6.6 (moderate positive conviction)
EM Equity overweight 1.5pp — DCS 7.0 (moderate positive conviction)
Real Assets overweight 1.5pp — DCS 7.2 (moderate positive conviction)
Cash & Alts underweight 4.5pp — DCS 5.7 (moderate positive conviction)

construction.narrative: The portfolio is built bottom-up from 27 investable instruments, each grouped by investment role into one of eight sleeves, then into Growth / Diversifier / Cash blocks. Within-sleeve weights are strategic policy weights tied to each asset's role — not optimizer output, so every position can be explained by why it is held, not merely that the model selected it. The clearest test is the safe-haven sleeve: weighting it to nominal Treasury duration (rather than to whatever had the lowest volatility) means it gained +4.7% in the 2008 crisis instead of losing money. Sector, factor, country and broad-basket instruments remain in the governed registry as tilts, available when there is a view but kept out of the base allocation.

sleeves.label: US Equity
sleeves.thesis: Own the US equity market with a large-cap core for liquidity and quality, plus deliberate mid- and small-cap weight for breadth and the size premium. Policy weights approximate market-cap so the sleeve is the market, not an accidental size bet. Sectors/factors are tilts layered on top when there is a view.

instruments.rationale: S&P 500 -- the large-cap core; deepest, most liquid US exposure, longest clean history (1993). ~80% reflects large-cap's share of US market cap.
S&P MidCap 400 -- mid-cap breadth and the size premium beyond the S&P 500.
Russell 2000 -- small-cap completion of the market-cap ladder; highest size/growth premium, sized modestly for its higher volatility.

sleeves.label: Intl Developed Equity
sleeves.thesis: Developed-ex-US equity weighted across the major regions roughly by market size (Europe largest, then Japan), with Australia and Canada added for Pacific and commodity-linked developed diversification. Intentional regional exposure, not a single broad-EAFE proxy.

instruments.rationale: FTSE Developed Europe -- the largest DM-ex-US region; ~55% reflects Europe's share of developed-ex-US market cap.
Japan -- the largest single DM-ex-US country (~28% of the region).
Australia -- Pacific-ex-Japan developed and commodity-linked exposure.
Canada -- developed-ex-US, commodity-tilted; added deliberately for resource-linked DM diversification (note: not part of EAFE).

sleeves.label: EM Equity
sleeves.thesis: Emerging-market equity across the largest country markets -- China (capped to limit single-country concentration), Taiwan, Korea, India and LatAm (Brazil, Mexico). Cap-aware with a China cap and genuine LatAm diversification. India is a core holding, phased in from its 2012 ETF inception (no synthetic backfill); the other legs cover the sleeve until then.

instruments.rationale: China large-cap -- the largest EM market, CAPPED at 30% to avoid the single-country concentration a pure cap-weight would impose.
Taiwan -- major EM-Asia / semiconductor exposure.
Korea -- major EM-Asia industrial/tech exposure.

India -- ~18% of MSCI EM and a structural growth market; now a CORE holding rather than an omission. Its ETF history starts 2012, so it is a documented PHASE-IN: the other five country legs are renormalized to cover the sleeve until 2012-03, after which India joins at its policy weight. No synthetic/backfilled data is used.
Brazil -- the largest LatAm market, commodity-linked EM diversification.
Mexico -- LatAm, US-linked manufacturing; rounds out EM diversification.

sleeves.label: US Investment Grade
sleeves.thesis: High-grade carry. Corporate IG (LQD) is the primary spread exposure; agency MBS (MBB) is a diversifying securitized sleeve, sized as a minority because of its negative convexity -- deliberately NOT maximized just because it is lower-vol.

instruments.rationale: Broad USD investment-grade corporates -- the primary spread/credit exposure and the sleeve's core thesis.

Agency mortgage-backed securities -- a diversifying securitized sleeve (distinct prepayment/convexity risk). A 35% minority by design: NOT maximized despite its lower volatility, because negative convexity is a real risk a vol measure understates.

sleeves.label: High Yield / EM Debt
sleeves.thesis: Carry in benign regimes. US high-yield corporates are the primary exposure; EM USD sovereign debt is diversifying carry with different drivers. Treated as growth/equity-like risk, not fixed income.

instruments.rationale: Broad USD high-yield corporates -- the primary carry exposure.
EM hard-currency sovereign debt -- diversifying carry with different (rates + EM-spread) drivers than US HY corporates.

sleeves.label: Government / TIPS
sleeves.thesis: The safe-haven DURATION anchor. Weighted toward nominal Treasuries (intermediate + long) for the flight-to-quality convexity that rallies when equities crash (2008, 2020), with TIPS a minority for inflation-regime protection. Deliberately NOT risk-parity-weighted, which would pile into low-vol TIPS/bills and strip the crisis hedge.

instruments.rationale: Intermediate nominal Treasuries -- the belly of the curve; the core rate-diversifying, flight-to-quality leg.

Long nominal Treasuries -- the maximum-convexity safe-haven leg (e.g. +34% in 2008). Deliberately a large weight: this is the crisis hedge the sleeve exists to provide, the opposite of what risk-parity would set.
Inflation-protected Treasuries -- real-rate / inflation-regime protection. A minority, because TIPS underperform nominals in a deflationary flight-to-quality (TIP -10% in the 2008 acute crisis).

sleeves.label: Real Assets / Commodities
sleeves.thesis: Real-asset and inflation diversification from distinct roles: gold as a monetary/crisis hedge, energy and agriculture as direct inflation hedges, REITs as real-asset income, silver as an industrial precious metal. Held as individual commodities, not one broad basket. Kept genuinely diversifying: gold-miner equity (GDX) is excluded from core because it carries equity beta, and the broad DBC basket is excluded to avoid double-counting components.

instruments.rationale: Physical gold -- the monetary/crisis store-of-value hedge; the largest single real-asset weight, the most reliable diversifier (held up in 2008) and genuinely equity-neutral (0.10 beta to SPY).
US REITs -- real-asset income and property exposure; a distinct hard-asset cash-flow stream.
Diversified energy (crude + products + gas) -- the broad energy inflation hedge.
Agricultural commodities -- a distinct, low-correlation real-asset inflation hedge.
WTI crude -- a direct single-commodity energy exposure within the energy allocation (owner: hold oil individually, not only via a basket).
Silver -- a precious metal with an industrial-demand tilt distinct from gold; higher volatility, sized smallest.

sleeves.label: Cash
sleeves.thesis: Dry powder and the risk-free anchor. FRED T-bill proxy pending a real cash total-return series (data-tier gap G-4).

instruments.rationale: FRED 3-month T-bill total return (TB3MS/12). Data-tier gap G-4: no investable BIL/SHV/SGOV total-return series in the universe yet.

flags.title: Safe-haven built for crisis, not low vol
flags.detail: The Govt sleeve is weighted to nominal-Treasury duration (IEF/TLT/TIP 40/35/25), not risk-parity. It returned +4.7% in the 2008 acute crisis (Sep–Nov), versus ~−5.8% under the prior HRP weighting that piled into low-vol TIPS.

flags.title: Gold via bullion, not miners
flags.detail: Gold-miner equity (GDX) is excluded from the Real Assets core — it carries 0.56 equity beta (vs gold's 0.10), which would contaminate a diversifier sleeve. Gold is held purely via GLD; GDX remains available as a tactical tilt.

flags.title: India is a core holding, phased in
flags.detail: India (~18% of MSCI EM) is a core 15% EM position, included from its 2012 ETF inception with the other country legs renormalised until then — no synthetic backfill is used.

flags.title: Cash is a proxy (G-4)
flags.detail: Cash uses a FRED T-bill total-return proxy pending a real investable cash series (CRSP/WRDS); the broad-commodity DBC basket is held only as a tilt to avoid double-counting its own oil/energy/agriculture components.

diversification.narrative: The book's diversification ratio is 1.21 (weighted-average sleeve volatility 12.8% vs realised portfolio volatility 10.6%) — a ratio above 1 means the sleeves are genuinely offsetting, not just averaging. Risk is spread across an effective 4.4 independent bets; the average pairwise correlation among held risk sleeves is 0.51. The heatmap shows where diversification is real (equities cluster; rates and gold are the genuine offsets).

risk.narrative: Forecast 36-month volatility is 9.1% against the 14.0% Neutral-stance budget (65.0% of budget used) — well within budget; no scaling applied. The equity factor drives 55.7% of total portfolio risk — typical for a multi-asset book. This is structural for an unlevered multi-asset portfolio of this equity weight (capital share understates risk share), and is monitored, not a breach: the only way to materially lower it is risk-parity-style construction.

risk_detail.narrative: Risk decomposition for both portfolios on the same covariance. The dynamic portfolio (DAA) runs at 10.6% vol vs SAA's 9.9%, spread across an effective 4.4 bets. Growth assets carry 81.2% of risk on 55.3% of capital — the structural equity tilt of an unlevered multi-asset book. Marginal contribution (MCTR) shows which sleeve a marginal dollar adds the most risk to.

track_record.verdict:
The tactical overlay decisively beats its own policy. Versus the static 8-sleeve policy (same universe, no tactical activity), the full system lifts Sharpe from 0.46 to 0.57, roughly doubles Calmar (0.19→0.36), and nearly halves max drawdown (-37.6%→-19.7%) — Layers 2–4 earn their complexity.

It does not out-Sharpe US 60/40 (0.57 vs 0.65), but that gap lives in the un-optimized base allocation — the diversified policy itself Sharpes only 0.46 because non-US and real assets lagged US equities over 2008–2026. Closing it is the house-CMA decision (G-2), not a layer fix.

Where it wins: tail risk. The system has the best max drawdown (-19.7%) and Calmar (0.36) of every option, 60/40 included. The value proposition is risk-managed compounding, not Sharpe-maximisation in a US-dominated decade.

BL anchor (now live): the Black-Litterman / building-block-CMA anchor backtests 0.56 Sharpe — slightly below the PC16 anchor (0.57) and at lower vol (9.0% vs 10.2%). It is forward-grounded, not backtest-tuned: its yield-driven CMA tilts more conservative (IG-heavy, less equity) than the equity bull rewarded. It does not close the 60/40 gap (that is US-equity-dominance, which no valuation-agnostic CMA tilts into). Anchor choice is one-line reversible — an evidenced owner decision.

comparison.narrative: What the dynamic process added. Over 2008–2026 the dynamic portfolio (DAA) lifted Sharpe by +0.10 (to 0.57) and cut max drawdown by 18pp (to -19.7%) versus the static strategic policy (SAA), at -2.0pp vol. The dynamic layers (regime overlay, tactical tilts, risk budget) are a risk-management overlay: the value is a shallower path, not extra return.

stress.narrative: Across recognised stress episodes the full system consistently cushions the drawdown versus all-equity and usually versus 60/40. In the 2022 rate shock it returned -18.0% vs -24.0% all-equity — the regime overlay and duration-anchored safe haven doing their job. This is historical realised performance over each window, net of cost — not a hypothetical.

scenarios.name: GFC replay
scenarios.desc: 2008-style: equities crater, flight to quality, credit blows out

scenarios.name: COVID crash replay
scenarios.desc: Fast equity shock + bond rally + credit stress

scenarios.name: Inflation shock
scenarios.desc: Commodities surge, rates rise, equities wobble

scenarios.name: Credit-spread widening
scenarios.desc: Risk-off in credit, mild equity drawdown

stress_forward.narrative: Forward-looking stress: each scenario is a set of factor shocks applied through each sleeve's estimated factor betas — an ex-ante estimate on today's weights, not a historical replay. Use the custom panel to set your own shocks. Important: this holds weights fixed, so SAA and DAA differ only by the current tactical tilt (the dynamic book is mildly risk-on now, so it can read marginally worse here). It does not capture the regime overlay's de-risking, which triggers as a crisis unfolds — that dynamic cushioning is what the historical stress tests (Overview) actually show (e.g. GFC: DAA −17.7% vs 60/40 −29.1%).

scenarios.narrative: Positioning is regime-driven and pre-committed. A confirmed downgrade to Slowdown would shift to the Defensive stance (growth assets cut to ~45%, Govt/Cash raised); a Contraction would trigger Max Defensive (growth ~26%). Two asymmetric overrides can force defense ahead of the 2-month confirmation: the safe-haven trigger (MRS < −1.0) and the drawdown-breach protocol (at 100% of the stance drawdown budget). Nothing can force the portfolio more aggressive than the regime signal implies.

return_attr.narrative: The dynamic book's return is overwhelmingly a growth-asset story: the Growth block contributed 5.9%/yr of the 7.5%/yr additive total (78%), led by US Equity (+370bp/yr). The largest drag was Cash & Alts (+10bp/yr). Bars are the annualized contribution of each sleeve and sum exactly to the additive total return; diversifiers contribute less return by design — their job is risk, shown in the risk-attribution tab.

risk_attr.narrative: Risk is far more concentrated than capital. The Growth block holds 71% of capital but 88% of portfolio risk; through the macro-factor lens a single factor — equity — drives 69% of variance. This is the structural signature of an unlevered multi-asset book and is monitored, not breached. Diversifiers earn their place here: they contribute little return but absorb the equity shock (see the SAA-vs-DAA risk and the stress tabs).

active_attr.narrative: What the dynamic process added — and how. Versus the static policy (same sleeves, same sleeve returns; only the weights differ), the dynamic book delivered +18bp/yr of net compound outperformance and cut max drawdown from ~38% to ~20%. The decomposition is revealing: the three active levers are nearly return-neutral on an arithmetic basis (regime stance +0, tactical tilt +10, risk budget -11 bp/yr) and trading cost is a -7bp drag — so the overlay does not win by picking winners. Its real lever is volatility reduction: cutting vol from 12.2% to 10.2% lifts compounding by +25bp/yr (less variance drag), which is what turns a roughly flat arithmetic active into a positive compound one. That is the honest signature of a risk-managed overlay: it earns through a shallower path, not through extra gross return. By sleeve, the active bet that paid most was US Inv. Grade (+18bp arithmetic).

benchmark_attr.narrative: Against 60/40, the dynamic book runs a structurally lower equity beta (0.5 vs 0.61) and broader diversification. Through a common macro-factor lens, the realized active return of -0.9%/yr breaks into a factor-timing component (-40bp — dominated by the equity-beta gap in a decade where US equity led) and an allocation residual (-46bp). The honest read: 60/40's higher US-equity beta was rewarded over 2008–2026, so a more diversified, lower-beta book gives up return for the shallower drawdowns it delivers — the same CMA/US-dominance reality flagged on the Performance tab.

regime_attr.narrative: The overlay's value is asymmetric and tail-concentrated — exactly where drawdowns are made. In the worst-decile equity months (22 months, S&P ≤ -6%) the dynamic process added +1884bp/yr of active return — the de-risking and duration-anchored safe haven doing their job when it counts. It gives a little back in calm and rising markets, which is the premium paid for that protection. Note that conditioning on macro regime alone understates this: bad-regime windows (-104bp average) include violent bear-market rebounds in which a de-risked book lags, so the protection is better seen against realized equity stress than against the macro label. This is why the headline value of the dynamic process is drawdown reduction, not average active return.

matrix.narrative: Where each book stands on the same axes. The scatter is the efficient-frontier view — up-and-left is better (more return per unit of risk); bubble size is max drawdown. The grid ranks every portfolio and benchmark on each metric (greener = better in that column). DAA sits at the low-volatility, shallow-drawdown corner; S&P 500 earns the most return but at the deepest drawdown; 60/40 is the Sharpe leader in this US-led decade. The dynamic book's case is the drawdown and Calmar columns, not the CAGR column.

monte_carlo.narrative: Over a 10-year horizon, block-bootstrapped from the realised return history, the dynamic book's median outcome is $2.02 per $1 invested (a 7.3%/yr median CAGR), with a 5–95% cone of $1.21–$3.20. The probability of doubling is 52%; of finishing below the starting stake, 1%. A drawdown worse than 20% occurs in 31% of paths (median worst drawdown -17%). On paired draws (same sampled months), the dynamic process finishes ahead of the static policy in 45% of paths — its edge is a shallower left tail (worst-5% drawdown -30% vs SAA -47%), not a higher median (SAA median $2.05).
